questions.question: The City Market sold 3, 5, 7, and 9 apples on four days. What is the mean number of apples sold per day?
Five market stalls earned 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 coins today. What is the mean earnings per stall?
What does the mean represent in a data set?
options: The sum divided by the count
questions.correct_answer: The sum divided by the count
questions.explanation: Mean = Σx ÷ n. It spreads the total equally across all values.

questions.question: The market recorded daily visitors: 120, 95, 110, 130, and 145. What is the mean number of visitors?
A city market stall had sales of 8, 12, 15, and x coins. If the mean is 12, what is x?
If you add a new value equal to the current mean to a data set, how does the mean change?
questions.explanation: Adding a value equal to the mean does not change the mean, because it pulls neither up nor down.

questions.question: A baker at the City Market baked 14, 18, 22, 10, and 16 loaves over 5 days. What is the daily mean?
Three market stalls have a mean revenue of 50 coins. Two more stalls join with revenues of 80 and 20 coins. What is the new mean?
questions.explanation: Original 3 stalls total = 150. New total = 150+80+20 = 250. New mean = 250÷5 = 50. The two new values average to 50 (the same as the original mean), so no change.

questions.question: The City Market's weekly revenue had a mean of 200 coins over 4 weeks. In week 5, revenue was 300 coins. What is the new 5-week mean?
questions.explanation: Total for 4 weeks = 4×200 = 800. New total = 800+300 = 1100. Mean = 1100÷5 = 220

questions.question: An outlier value of 500 is added to a small data set {10, 12, 11}. What effect does it most likely have on the mean?
questions.explanation: The mean is sensitive to outliers. New mean = (10+12+11+500) ÷ 4 = 533÷4 ≈ 133, far above the original ~11.

questions.question: The City Library has shelves with 3, 7, 2, 9, and 5 books. What is the median number of books?
questions.explanation: Sort: 2, 3, 5, 7, 9. The middle value (3rd of 5) is 5.

questions.question: What is the first step to finding the median of a data set?
questions.explanation: The median is the middle value, so values must be ordered first.

questions.question: Library visitors on 5 days: 10, 40, 20, 30, 50. What is the median?
questions.explanation: Sorted: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Middle value = 30.

questions.question: A library has 6 sections with 4, 8, 2, 6, 10, and 14 books. What is the median?
questions.explanation: Sorted: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14. Even count → median = (6+8)÷2 = 7.

questions.question: Which measure is better for representing a typical house price in a city where most homes cost 300K but a few sell for 10M?
questions.explanation: Extreme values (outliers) pull the mean far from typical. The median is resistant to outliers.

questions.question: City Library checkout times in minutes: 2, 5, 3, 8, 1, 4. What is the median?
If a new value is added that is larger than the current median, how does the median change?
options: Stays the same or increases slightly
questions.explanation: Adding a large value shifts positions, and the new median depends on the full distribution and whether n is now odd or even.

questions.question: Scores of 7 students: 45, 72, 88, 91, 55, 63, 77. What is the median score?
questions.explanation: Sorted: 45, 55, 63, 72, 77, 88, 91. The 4th of 7 values = 72.

questions.question: The library has 8 rooms with areas: 12, 20, 8, 35, 15, 28, 10, 22 m². What is the median area?
A dataset has a mean of 50 and a median of 30. What does this suggest about the distribution?
questions.explanation: When mean > median, large outliers pull the mean right. The distribution has a long right tail.

questions.question: What does standard deviation measure?
options: How spread out values are from the mean
questions.correct_answer: How spread out values are from the mean
questions.explanation: Standard deviation quantifies variability — how much individual values differ from the mean.

questions.question: Weather Tower recorded daily temperatures: 20, 20, 20, 20°C all week. What is the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: All values are identical, so there is no spread. Standard deviation = 0.

questions.question: Which data set has a higher standard deviation?
questions.explanation: The values 8 and 12 are farther from the mean (10) than in the other sets, so this set has greater spread.

questions.question: Temperatures recorded at the Weather Tower: 15, 17, 19, 21, 23°C. The mean is 19°C. Which value contributes most to the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: 15 is farthest from the mean (19), with deviation = 4. All other deviations are ≤ 2.

questions.question: A weather forecast has low standard deviation. This means the predictions are:
options: Consistent and close to the mean
questions.correct_answer: Consistent and close to the mean
questions.explanation: Low standard deviation → values cluster tightly around the mean → more consistent and predictable.

questions.question: If every temperature in a data set is increased by 5°C, what happens to the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Shifting all values by a constant doesn't change the spread between them. Standard deviation measures relative spread.

questions.question: City A has rainfall standard deviation of 2mm; City B has 8mm. Which city has more predictable rainfall?
questions.explanation: Lower standard deviation = less variability = more predictable. City A's rainfall varies less from its mean.

questions.question: A data set has values {2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9}. The mean is 5. What is the variance?
questions.explanation: Deviations from mean: -3,-1,-1,-1,0,0,2,4. Squared: 9,1,1,1,0,0,4,16. Sum=32. Variance=32÷8=4.

questions.question: If a data set has variance of 25, what is the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Standard deviation = √variance = √25 = 5.

questions.question: In a normal distribution, approximately what percentage of data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean?
questions.explanation: The 68-95-99.7 rule: ~68% within 1σ, ~95% within 2σ, ~99.7% within 3σ.

questions.question: At the Casino & Event Hall, a spinner has 4 equal sections: red, blue, green, yellow. What is the probability of landing on blue?
questions.explanation: Probability = favorable outcomes ÷ total outcomes = 1÷4 = 0.25

questions.question: What is the probability of a certain event (something that must happen)?
questions.explanation: A certain event has probability 1 — it always occurs. Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

questions.question: A bag has 3 red balls and 7 blue balls. What is the probability of picking a red ball?
At the event hall, a fair die is rolled. What is the probability of getting a number greater than 4?
questions.explanation: Numbers greater than 4 on a die: {5, 6} = 2 outcomes. P = 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.question: Two fair coins are flipped. What is the probability of getting exactly one head?
questions.explanation: Sample space: HH, HT, TH, TT. Exactly one head: HT, TH = 2 outcomes. P = 2/4 = 1/2.

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.4 and A and B are complementary events, what is P(B)?
questions.explanation: Complementary events sum to 1: P(B) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6.

questions.question: The casino has a game where P(win) = 0.2. If you play 10 times, how many wins do you expect?
questions.explanation: Expected value = n × P = 10 × 0.2 = 2 expected wins.

questions.question: From a deck of 52 cards, what is the probability of drawing an ace or a heart?
questions.explanation: P(A∪B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B) = 4/52 + 13/52 - 1/52 = 16/52 (the ace of hearts counted twice).

questions.question: Two events are independent: P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.5. What is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: For independent events: P(A∩B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.3 × 0.5 = 0.15.

questions.question: In a city of 1000, 200 own a bicycle and 150 own a scooter; 50 own both. What is the probability a random resident owns at least one?
The City Bank uses regression to predict loan amounts from salary data. What does a linear regression line do?
options: Fits the best straight line through data points
Counts how many data points exist
questions.correct_answer: Fits the best straight line through data points
questions.explanation: Linear regression finds the line that minimizes the total squared distance between the line and all data points.

questions.question: In the regression equation ŷ = a + bx, what does b represent?
questions.explanation: b is the slope: for every 1-unit increase in x, y changes by b units. a is the y-intercept.

questions.question: A bank's regression line shows that as loan size increases, repayment time also increases. This is called:
questions.explanation: When both variables increase together, the slope is positive — a positive relationship.

questions.question: The City Bank regression model: ŷ = 5000 + 200x, where x is years of employment. Predict the loan amount for 8 years of employment.
A bank analyst finds r² = 0.64 for a salary-to-savings regression. What does this mean?
options: 64% of the variation in savings is explained by salary
questions.correct_answer: 64% of the variation in savings is explained by salary
questions.explanation: r² (coefficient of determination) tells us what percentage of variation in y is explained by x. Here, salary explains 64% of savings variation.

questions.question: A customer's actual savings are 800 coins but the regression predicts 750. What is the residual?
questions.explanation: Residual = actual − predicted = 800 − 750 = 50. Positive residual means the actual value is above the line.

questions.question: The regression line always passes through which point?
options: The point (x̄, ȳ) — the means of x and y
questions.correct_answer: The point (x̄, ȳ) — the means of x and y
questions.explanation: A key property of least-squares regression: the line always passes through (x̄, ȳ), the intersection of the two means.

questions.question: Bank data: mean salary = 50K, mean savings = 10K, r = 0.8, SD(salary) = 10K, SD(savings) = 2K. What is the regression slope b?
questions.explanation: b = r × (SD_y / SD_x) = 0.8 × (2/10) = 0.8 × 0.2 = 0.16. Each extra 1K salary corresponds to 0.16K more in savings.

questions.question: A bank uses a regression model trained on data from 20–50 year-olds to predict savings for 80-year-olds. Why is this risky?
options: This is extrapolation beyond the data range
Regression only works for younger people
questions.correct_answer: This is extrapolation beyond the data range
questions.explanation: Extrapolation applies the model outside the range of x values it was built on. Patterns may change, making predictions unreliable.

questions.question: A regression model has r² = 0.09. What does this tell a bank analyst?
options: The predictor explains only 9% of variation — a weak model
questions.correct_answer: The predictor explains only 9% of variation — a weak model
questions.explanation: r² = 0.09 means the independent variable explains only 9% of variance in the outcome. Other factors drive most of the variation.

questions.question: The WaffleStack Market analyzes prices and sales. What does correlation measure?
options: The strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables
questions.correct_answer: The strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables
questions.explanation: Correlation (Pearson r) ranges from −1 to +1 and measures how strongly two variables are linearly related.

questions.question: A market study finds r = 1 between advertising spend and sales. What does this mean?
questions.explanation: r = 1 means every data point falls exactly on a line with positive slope — a perfect positive correlation.

questions.question: A market analyst finds r ≈ 0 between shoe size and test scores. What does this mean?
questions.explanation: r ≈ 0 indicates no meaningful linear relationship between the two variables.

questions.question: The market finds r = −0.85 between item price and units sold. What does this indicate?
options: Higher price → more units sold (strong positive)
Higher price → fewer units sold (strong negative)
No relationship between price and sales
questions.correct_answer: Higher price → fewer units sold (strong negative)
questions.explanation: r = −0.85 is a strong negative correlation: as price increases, units sold decreases. Not perfect (not −1) but strong.

questions.question: A market study shows that ice cream sales and drowning rates are both high in summer (r = 0.9). Does ice cream cause drowning?
options: Yes, ice cream increases drowning risk
No — correlation does not imply causation; a third factor (hot weather) explains both
Yes — a correlation above 0.8 always implies causation
No — r must equal 1 before causation applies
questions.correct_answer: No — correlation does not imply causation; a third factor (hot weather) explains both
questions.explanation: Correlation shows association, not cause. Here hot weather drives both variables — a classic example of a confounding variable.

questions.question: Which correlation shows a stronger linear relationship: r = 0.3 or r = −0.8?
options: r = −0.8, because its absolute value is larger
Cannot compare positive and negative correlations
questions.correct_answer: r = −0.8, because its absolute value is larger
questions.explanation: Strength is measured by |r|. |−0.8| = 0.8 > 0.3, so r = −0.8 indicates a stronger (though negative) relationship.

questions.question: A market scatter plot shows points tightly clustered around a downward-sloping line. The correlation r is most likely:
questions.explanation: Tight clustering around a downward line means a strong negative linear relationship → r close to −1.

questions.question: Market data has r = 0.6. What percentage of variance in sales is explained by the price variable?
questions.explanation: r² = 0.6² = 0.36 = 36%. The coefficient of determination r² tells the proportion of variance explained.

questions.question: A researcher adds a confounding variable to the analysis and finds the r between advertising and sales drops from 0.8 to 0.2. What most likely happened?
options: The confounding variable was driving most of the relationship
Correlation cannot decrease when variables are added
The sample size was too small
questions.correct_answer: The confounding variable was driving most of the relationship
questions.explanation: When controlling for a confounder reduces r, it shows the confounder was responsible for much of the apparent correlation.

questions.question: A market researcher notes that the relationship between study hours and exam scores looks curved (not linear) on a scatter plot. Why might Pearson r be misleading here?
options: Pearson r requires at least 100 data points
Pearson r only measures linear relationships; a curved pattern can show r ≈ 0 even with a strong association
Pearson r cannot handle exam score data
questions.correct_answer: Pearson r only measures linear relationships; a curved pattern can show r ≈ 0 even with a strong association
questions.explanation: Pearson r detects linear association only. A strong non-linear (curved) relationship can yield r near 0, hiding the true pattern.

questions.question: City Hall runs elections where each voter votes YES or NO. Which condition makes this a binomial experiment?
options: Each trial has more than 2 outcomes
Each trial has exactly 2 outcomes (success/failure), trials are independent, and p is fixed
The outcomes must be equally likely
There must be at least 100 trials
questions.correct_answer: Each trial has exactly 2 outcomes (success/failure), trials are independent, and p is fixed
questions.explanation: A binomial experiment requires: exactly 2 outcomes per trial, fixed number of trials (n), constant probability (p), and independent trials.

questions.question: City Hall surveys n = 10 residents; each has p = 0.5 chance of supporting the new park. What is the expected (mean) number of supporters?
questions.explanation: Mean of binomial = n × p = 10 × 0.5 = 5 expected supporters.

questions.question: In a binomial experiment, 'success' means:
options: Always a positive outcome in real life
The specific outcome we are counting
Getting a value above the mean
questions.correct_answer: The specific outcome we are counting
questions.explanation: 'Success' is just a label for the outcome we're tracking — it can be any defined event (e.g., voting YES, a defective product, heads on a coin).

questions.question: City Hall polls n = 5 residents; p = 0.4 that each supports a new bus route. Using B(5, 0.4), what is P(X = 2)?
For a binomial distribution B(n, p), what is the standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Variance = np(1−p). Standard deviation = √(np(1−p)).

questions.question: Which scenario is NOT a binomial experiment?
options: Flipping a coin 20 times and counting heads
Rolling a die 10 times and counting sixes
Testing 100 voters: support vs. oppose a policy
questions.explanation: Height is a continuous variable with many outcomes — not binary (success/failure). The other scenarios all have exactly two outcomes per trial.

questions.question: City Hall finds that 10% of building permits have errors. Out of 20 permits, what is the expected number with errors?
questions.explanation: E(X) = np = 20 × 0.10 = 2 permits with errors expected.

questions.question: City Hall finds that 30% of residents recycle. In a sample of 4 residents, what is P(at least 1 recycles)?
In B(3, 0.6), what is P(X = 3) — all 3 residents support the motion?
When can a binomial distribution be approximated by a normal distribution?
questions.explanation: The normal approximation to the binomial is valid when both np ≥ 10 and nq ≥ 10 (sufficient expected successes and failures).

questions.question: The Research Institute tests whether a new study method improves scores. What is the null hypothesis (H₀) in this case?
options: The new method has no effect on scores
The new method is the best method
questions.correct_answer: The new method has no effect on scores
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis (H₀) always states no effect or no difference. We test whether the data gives enough evidence to reject it.

questions.question: The Research Institute gets a p-value of 0.03. What does this mean?
options: There is a 3% chance the null hypothesis is true
If H₀ is true, there is a 3% chance of observing results this extreme or more
The experiment has a 3% error rate
questions.correct_answer: If H₀ is true, there is a 3% chance of observing results this extreme or more
questions.explanation: The p-value is the probability of getting results at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true. It is NOT the probability that H₀ is true.

questions.question: Researchers set significance level α = 0.05. This means they are willing to accept a ___% chance of a false positive.
questions.explanation: α = 0.05 means the researchers accept a 5% chance of incorrectly rejecting a true null hypothesis (Type I error).

questions.question: The Research Institute obtains p = 0.03 and set α = 0.05. What should they conclude?
questions.explanation: When p < α (0.03 < 0.05), the result is statistically significant and we reject the null hypothesis.

questions.question: A researcher rejects H₀ when it is actually true. This is called:
questions.explanation: Type I error = rejecting a true H₀. Its probability equals α. Also called a false positive.

questions.question: Researchers fail to reject H₀ even though the new study method actually works. This is called:
questions.explanation: Type II error = failing to reject a false H₀. Its probability is called β. Also called a false negative.

questions.question: The Research Institute tests whether a drug changes blood pressure (could increase or decrease). What type of test is appropriate?
questions.explanation: A two-tailed test checks for any difference (increase or decrease). Use it when the direction of the effect is not specified in advance.

questions.question: A sample of 36 students has mean score 72, SD = 12. H₀: μ = 75. Calculate the z test statistic.
A research lab runs 20 independent tests at α = 0.05. Even if all null hypotheses are true, how many false positives are expected?
questions.explanation: Expected false positives = 20 × 0.05 = 1. This is the multiple testing problem — running many tests inflates false discovery rates.

questions.question: A study has statistical power of 0.80. What does this mean?
options: There is an 80% chance H₀ is true
There is an 80% chance of correctly rejecting a false H₀
questions.correct_answer: There is an 80% chance of correctly rejecting a false H₀
questions.explanation: Power = 1 − β = probability of correctly detecting a real effect. Power of 0.80 is generally considered acceptable in research design.

questions.question: The City Survey Center wants to know what all 10,000 residents think. They ask 500 people. What are those 500 people called?
questions.explanation: A sample is a subset of the population selected for study. The full group of 10,000 is the population.

questions.question: Which sampling method gives every member of the population an equal chance of being selected?
questions.explanation: Random (or simple random) sampling ensures each member has an equal probability of selection, reducing bias.

questions.question: The City Survey Center only surveys people in one specific neighborhood. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Surveying only one neighborhood excludes other residents, making the sample unrepresentative of the whole city.

questions.question: A city has 60% adults and 40% youth. A stratified sample of 100 should have how many adults?
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling preserves population proportions: 60% of 100 = 60 adults.

questions.question: A survey finds that 42% of 400 sampled residents prefer the new park design. What is 42% called in this context?
questions.explanation: A statistic describes the sample. A parameter describes the full population. Since 42% comes from 400 people (sample), it's a statistic.

questions.question: As sample size increases, what generally happens to the accuracy of estimates?
questions.explanation: Larger samples reduce sampling error and produce estimates closer to the true population parameter.

questions.question: Surveyors number all 500 residents 1–500, then select every 10th person. This is called:
questions.explanation: Selecting every kth element from an ordered list is systematic sampling.

questions.question: A sample of 100 gives a mean of 75 with standard error of 5. What is the 95% confidence interval (using z=2)?
A survey of 1,000 finds 55% prefer option A. What is the margin of error at 95% confidence (SE = √(p(1-p)/n))?
A city survey has selection bias because only online respondents were included. The city has 30% non-internet users. This will most likely:
questions.explanation: Online surveys systematically exclude non-internet users, making the sample biased toward connected residents.

questions.question: The WaffleStack News Station reports a 95% confidence interval for average daily readership. What is a confidence interval?
options: A range of plausible values for a population parameter
questions.correct_answer: A range of plausible values for a population parameter
questions.explanation: A confidence interval gives a range of values that is likely to contain the true population parameter, based on sample data.

questions.question: A news poll reports: '95% CI = [42%, 48%] for voter support.' What does '95%' mean?
options: If we repeated this process many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true proportion
We are 95% certain this specific interval is correct
The poll has a 95% response rate
questions.correct_answer: If we repeated this process many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true proportion
questions.explanation: 95% confidence means the procedure produces intervals that capture the true parameter 95% of the time — not that this specific interval has 95% probability of being correct.

questions.question: A news station increases from 90% to 99% confidence level (same data). What happens to the interval?
questions.explanation: Higher confidence requires capturing more possible values → a wider interval. There is a direct trade-off between confidence level and precision.

questions.question: A news survey finds mean readership = 500, SE = 25. Using z = 2 for 95% confidence, what is the 95% CI?
The news station doubles its sample size (same population). What happens to the confidence interval width?
questions.explanation: SE = σ/√n. Doubling n multiplies √n by √2, so SE decreases by √2 → CI width decreases by factor √2 ≈ 1.41.

questions.question: A 95% CI for a new policy effect is [−0.5, 3.2] points. Can the station conclude the policy significantly increased scores?
options: Yes — the upper bound is positive
No — the interval includes 0, so no effect is plausible
Yes — most of the interval is positive
No — the interval is too wide
questions.correct_answer: No — the interval includes 0, so no effect is plausible
questions.explanation: When a CI includes 0 (no effect), we cannot conclude significance at that confidence level — 0 is a plausible value for the true effect.

questions.question: A news poll reports ±3% margin of error. What is the margin of error part of the confidence interval formula?
options: z × SE (the half-width of the CI)
questions.correct_answer: z × SE (the half-width of the CI)
questions.explanation: Margin of error = z × SE. It is the half-width added and subtracted from the sample statistic to form the CI.

questions.question: A survey of 400 residents finds 52% support a policy. Calculate the 95% CI for the true proportion (z = 1.96).
A 99% CI is [48, 62] and a 95% CI from the same data is [50, 60]. Which is wider and why?
options: The 95% CI is wider — higher confidence requires more precision
The 99% CI is wider — higher confidence requires a wider range to be more sure
Width depends only on sample size
questions.correct_answer: The 99% CI is wider — higher confidence requires a wider range to be more sure
questions.explanation: 99% CI uses z = 2.576 vs z = 1.96 for 95%. A higher confidence level always produces a wider interval.

questions.question: A 95% CI test and a two-sided hypothesis test at α = 0.05 are run on the same data. If the CI excludes the null value, what can you conclude about the p-value?
options: Nothing — CI and hypothesis tests are unrelated
questions.explanation: There is an exact duality: a 95% CI excludes the null value if and only if a two-sided test at α = 0.05 rejects H₀ (p < 0.05).
